humanities_role_explanation: SimpleQA contains general-knowledge factual questions which include humanities content, such as history, literature, art, and popular culture (e.g., TV shows and video games) as factual QA items.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: A text-only dataset of short, fact-seeking queries. Model completions are evaluated against a ground-truth answer using an LLM classifier.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates the model's factual retrieval/recognition and its calibration (the alignment of its stated confidence or output frequency with its actual accuracy).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed as a general-purpose factuality benchmark spanning various topics from science to entertainment.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated OpenAI models including GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, o1-preview, and o1-mini.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLM is the primary system being evaluated on factuality and calibration.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes subjects like History, Literature, TV shows, and Video Games, which map to standard ASJC arts and humanities disciplines.

quantitative_metrics.note: Measures correctness rate (accuracy), incorrectness/hallucination rate, 'not attempted' rate, and calibration curves.

english_language.note: The paper and the SimpleQA dataset are in English.

summary: SimpleQA is a text-based, short-form factual question-answering benchmark designed to measure factuality and calibration in frontier language models. It contains 4,326 high-quality, indisputable questions verified by multiple independent AI trainers to ensure a low error rate of approximately 3%. The benchmark evaluates model correctness, incorrect/hallucination rates, and how well models recognize their own knowledge limits.
key_findings: Frontier models struggle on SimpleQA, with GPT-4o scoring under 40% accuracy. Reasoning models (o1-preview and o1-mini) choose to 'not attempt' questions more frequently than non-reasoning models (GPT-4o), showing better calibration. However, all models still consistently overstate their confidence.
